expanded_description: Alkynes are unsaturated hydrocarbons containing at least one carbon-carbon triple bond ($\text{C}\equiv\text{C}$). This section explores their unique linear structure, $sp$ hybridization, and systematic IUPAC nomenclature rules that distinguish them from alkanes and alkenes. Alkynes represent the most unsaturated family of simple hydrocarbons.

core_concept.principle: The Carbon-Carbon Triple Bond Defines Alkyne Structure and Reactivity
core_concept.explanation: Alkynes contain one or more $\text{C}\equiv\text{C}$ triple bonds, consisting of one strong $\sigma$ (sigma) bond and two weaker $\pi$ (pi) bonds. The two $\pi$-bonds form from sideways overlap of two pairs of unhybridized $p$ orbitals, creating cylindrical electron density around the bond axis. This structure makes alkynes the most unsaturated hydrocarbons, with linear geometry ($180^\circ$ bond angles) around triple-bonded carbons and distinctive reactivity patterns.

image.alt: Diagram showing alkyne triple bond with sigma and two pi components
image.caption: The $\text{C}\equiv\text{C}$ triple bond: one $\sigma$-bond (head-on overlap) + two $\pi$-bonds (perpendicular $p$-orbital overlaps).

sections.introduction: Alkynes are unsaturated hydrocarbons characterized by the presence of at least one carbon-carbon triple bond ($\text{C}\equiv\text{C}$). The term 'unsaturated' indicates that these molecules contain significantly fewer hydrogen atoms than the maximum possible for their carbon count, due to the presence of two $\pi$-bonds. Alkynes are sometimes called 'acetylenes' after the simplest member, acetylene (ethyne). They are less common in nature than alkanes and alkenes but are important industrial feedstocks and intermediates in organic synthesis. The triple bond makes alkynes the most electron-rich and reactive of the simple hydrocarbon families.

key_concept.definition: An alkyne is an unsaturated hydrocarbon containing at least one carbon-carbon triple bond, with general molecular formula $\text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n-2}$ for acyclic alkynes with one triple bond. Each triple-bonded carbon is $sp$ hybridized with linear geometry.
key_concept.context: The triple bond consists of one $\sigma$-bond and two $\pi$-bonds. The two $\pi$-bonds are oriented perpendicular to each other, creating cylindrical symmetry around the bond axis. This makes alkynes more reactive than both alkanes and alkenes toward addition reactions.

image.alt: General structure of alkynes showing triple bond
image.caption: Alkyne general structure: $\text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n-2}$ with at least one $\text{C}\equiv\text{C}$ triple bond.

principles.statement: For acyclic alkynes with exactly one triple bond, the general molecular formula is $\text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n-2}$, containing four fewer hydrogens than the corresponding alkane ($\text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n+2}$) and two fewer than the corresponding alkene ($\text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n}$).
principles.application: This formula allows quick identification of alkyne structures from molecular formulas. Each triple bond reduces hydrogen count by 4 relative to the saturated alkane (equivalent to two degrees of unsaturation). Multiple triple bonds further reduce hydrogen count: $\text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n-6}$ for two triple bonds (diynes).
principles.analogy: Think of saturation levels like water content: alkanes are fully soaked (maximum H), alkenes are half-squeezed (minus 2 H), and alkynes are thoroughly wrung out (minus 4 H). The more unsaturated, the more 'thirsty' the molecule is for addition reactions.
principles.example: Ethyne ($\text{C}_2\text{H}_2$): $n=2$, so $\text{H} = 2n-2 = 2$. Compare to ethane ($\text{C}_2\text{H}_6$): the triple bond removes 4 hydrogens. Compare to ethene ($\text{C}_2\text{H}_4$): the additional $\pi$-bond removes 2 more hydrogens.

principles.statement: The $sp$ hybridization of triple-bonded carbons creates linear geometry with bond angles of exactly $180^\circ$, making the $\text{C}-\text{C}\equiv\text{C}-\text{C}$ unit perfectly straight.
principles.application: This linear geometry affects molecular shape, packing in solids, and physical properties. Unlike alkenes (trigonal planar, $120^\circ$) and alkanes (tetrahedral, $109.5^\circ$), alkynes have no geometric isomerism around the triple bond because all four positions around the $\text{C}\equiv\text{C}$ unit are collinear.
principles.analogy: Imagine the triple bond as a rigid steel rod: everything attached to it must line up in a straight line. There's no bending or angling possible at the triple bond carbons.
principles.example: In but-2-yne ($\text{CH}_3-\text{C}\equiv\text{C}-\text{CH}_3$), all four carbons lie on a straight line. The $\text{C}-\text{C}\equiv\text{C}$ and $\text{C}\equiv\text{C}-\text{C}$ bond angles are both $180^\circ$.

components.definition: The alkyne functional group consists of two $sp$ hybridized carbon atoms connected by one $\sigma$-bond and two $\pi$-bonds, with bond angles of $180^\circ$ in linear geometry.
components.interpretation: The $\sigma$-bond ($\sim 350 \text{ kJ/mol}$) is the strongest component. Each $\pi$-bond ($\sim 245 \text{ kJ/mol}$) is weaker than the $\sigma$-bond but stronger than alkene $\pi$-bonds. Total triple bond energy is less than three times a single bond energy, making the $\pi$-bonds reactive sites for addition reactions.

image.alt: Diagram showing sigma and two pi bond components in alkyne
image.caption: Alkyne triple bond: $\sigma$-bond (blue, head-on overlap) + two perpendicular $\pi$-bonds (red and green, sideways $p$-orbital overlaps).

sections.introduction: Understanding alkyne structure requires detailed knowledge of $sp$ hybridization—the orbital mixing that creates the linear geometry around triple-bonded carbons. This hybridization pattern determines bond angles, molecular shape, and the formation of the two $\pi$-bonds that define alkyne reactivity and distinguish them from alkenes and alkanes.

classifications.value: Found in all carbon atoms participating in $\text{C}\equiv\text{C}$ triple bonds
characteristics: Two $sp$ hybrid orbitals + two unhybridized $p$ orbitals
Forms two $\pi$-bonds + one $\sigma$-bond per triple-bonded carbon
classifications.behavior: The two $sp$ orbitals are oriented $180^\circ$ apart (linear), forming $\sigma$-bonds along the bond axis. The two unhybridized $p$ orbitals are perpendicular to each other and to the bond axis, overlapping with neighboring $p$ orbitals to create two orthogonal $\pi$-bonds.

examples.explanation: Each carbon uses its $2s$ orbital and one $2p$ orbital to form two $sp$ hybrids. These form $\sigma$-bonds: one $\text{C}-\text{H}$ bond and one $\text{C}-\text{C}$ $\sigma$-bond. The two remaining unhybridized $2p$ orbitals on each carbon overlap to form two perpendicular $\pi$-bonds.

image.alt: Ethyne showing sp hybridization and orbital overlap
image.caption: Ethyne: each carbon is $sp$ hybridized with linear geometry ($180^\circ$ bond angles).

examples.explanation: The two triple-bonded carbons are $sp$ hybridized; the methyl carbon ($\text{CH}_3$) is $sp^3$ hybridized. This creates a molecule with mixed hybridization: linear at the triple bond, tetrahedral at the methyl group.

classifications.value: Head-on orbital overlap creating strong, localized bonds along the axis
characteristics: Electron density concentrated along bond axis
classifications.behavior: In alkynes, $\sigma$-bonds form the rigid linear skeleton: $\text{C}-\text{C}$ $\sigma$-bond between triple-bonded carbons, and $\text{C}-\text{H}$ or $\text{C}-\text{C}$ $\sigma$-bonds to attached substituents. All $\sigma$-bonds lie on the same axis.

examples.explanation: Three $\sigma$-bonds total: one $\text{C}-\text{C}$ ($sp$-$sp$) and two $\text{C}-\text{H}$ ($sp$-$s$). These create the rigid linear structure with all four atoms collinear.

image.caption: Ethyne $\sigma$-framework: all atoms lie on a straight line with $180^\circ$ bond angles.

classifications.value: Sideways $p$-orbital overlap creating cylindrical electron density around bond axis
characteristics: Formed by two pairs of unhybridized $p$-$p$ orbital overlaps
Electron density in cylindrical sheath around $\sigma$-bond
classifications.behavior: The two $\pi$-bonds form after the $\sigma$-bond framework is established. One $\pi$-bond forms from $p_y$-$p_y$ overlap, the other from $p_z$-$p_z$ overlap (perpendicular to each other). This creates cylindrical symmetry of electron density around the bond axis.

examples.explanation: Two perpendicular $2p$ orbital pairs overlap sideways, creating electron density in a cylindrical sheath around the $\sigma$-bond axis. This $\pi$-electron cloud is exposed and highly reactive toward electrophiles.

image.alt: Pi bonds showing perpendicular p-orbital overlaps
image.caption: Two $\pi$-bonds: perpendicular $p$-orbital overlaps create cylindrical electron density around the $\sigma$-bond axis.

sections.key_concept: Alkyne carbons are $sp$ hybridized: two $sp$ orbitals form $\sigma$-bonds in a line ($180^\circ$ angles); two unhybridized $p$ orbitals form two perpendicular $\pi$-bonds, creating cylindrical electron density.

sections.introduction: IUPAC nomenclature for alkynes follows similar principles to alkane and alkene naming but with critical modifications to indicate the presence and position of the triple bond. The suffix changes from '-ane' to '-yne', and the chain must be numbered to give the triple bond the lowest possible position number. When both double and triple bonds are present, special priority rules apply.

key_concept.definition: Alkyne IUPAC names follow the format: [Substituents]-[Triple Bond Position]-[Parent Chain]-yne. The parent chain must contain the triple bond, and numbering prioritizes the triple bond position over substituent positions.
key_concept.context: For alkynes with 2 or 3 carbons, position numbers are unnecessary (ethyne, propyne) because the triple bond can only be in one position. For 4+ carbons, the triple bond position must be specified. When both double and triple bonds exist, the suffix becomes '-enyne' with specific numbering rules.

image.caption: Alkyne naming: substituent positions + triple bond position + parent name with '-yne' suffix.

principles.statement: The parent chain must be the longest continuous carbon chain that contains the triple bond, even if a longer chain without the triple bond exists.
principles.application: This rule ensures the functional group (triple bond) is always part of the parent name. A 5-carbon chain with the triple bond takes priority over a 6-carbon chain without it. This is identical to alkene nomenclature rules.
principles.analogy: Think of the triple bond as the 'VIP' of the molecule—it must be included in the main party (parent chain) even if there's a bigger room (longer chain) available elsewhere. The functional group defines the molecule's identity.
principles.example: In a molecule with a 6-carbon chain (no triple bond) and a 5-carbon chain (with triple bond), the 5-carbon chain is the parent: pentyne, not hexane.

principles.principle: Numbering Priority: Triple Bond Over Substituents
principles.statement: The parent chain is numbered from the end that gives the triple bond the lowest possible position number, even if this results in higher substituent numbers.
principles.application: Triple bond position takes priority over substituent positions. Only when triple bond positions are equal from both ends do substituent positions determine numbering direction. This mirrors alkene numbering rules.
principles.analogy: Like addressing a letter: the street name (triple bond) is more important than the house number (substituent). Get the street right first, then optimize the house number.
principles.example: 4-methylpent-1-yne (triple bond at 1) is correct, NOT 2-methylpent-4-yne (triple bond at 4), even though 2 < 4 for the methyl group.

principles.statement: The suffix '-yne' replaces '-ane', and the triple bond position is indicated by a number before the suffix (e.g., pent-2-yne) or before the parent name (e.g., 2-pentyne).
principles.application: Current IUPAC recommendations prefer pent-2-yne format (position before suffix), but 2-pentyne is still widely accepted. For multiple triple bonds, use '-diyne', '-triyne', etc., with all positions specified.
principles.analogy: The '-yne' suffix is like a flag marking 'triple bond here!' The position number tells you exactly which carbon pair hosts this flag.
principles.example: But-1-yne ($\text{HC}\equiv\text{C}-\text{CH}_2-\text{CH}_3$) vs but-2-yne ($\text{CH}_3-\text{C}\equiv\text{C}-\text{CH}_3$)—different compounds with different properties.

principles.statement: When both double and triple bonds are present, the suffix is '-enyne' (not '-yneene'). Numbering gives the lowest position to whichever multiple bond is nearest the end, but if equidistant, the double bond receives lower numbering.
principles.application: This creates systematic names like pent-3-en-1-yne or hex-1-en-5-yne. The 'e' from '-ene' is dropped before '-yne' for easier pronunciation. Double bond gets priority in tiebreaker situations.
principles.analogy: Think of it as a race: both bonds want the lowest number. If they're equally close to the ends, the double bond ('ene') wins the tiebreaker and gets the lower number.
principles.example: $\text{HC}\equiv\text{C}-\text{CH}=\text{CH}-\text{CH}_3$: Number from left gives triple bond at 1, double at 3. Name: pent-3-en-1-yne (not pent-2-en-4-yne).

worked_examples.title: Naming a Simple Alkyne

step_by_step.explanation: Identify the longest chain containing the triple bond.
Number from the end giving the triple bond the lowest position.
step_by_step.calculation: $\text{Left-to-right: triple bond at 3. Right-to-left: triple bond at 2. Choose 2.}$

step_by_step.explanation: Check for substituents: none present in this molecule.
Assemble the name with triple bond position.

worked_examples.final_answer: pent-2-yne. The triple bond is at position 2, giving the lowest possible number.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Triple bond position takes priority in numbering; suffix '-yne' indicates alkyne family.

worked_examples.title: Naming a Branched Alkyne

step_by_step.explanation: Find longest chain containing the triple bond.
Number for lowest triple bond position.
step_by_step.calculation: $\text{Left-to-right: triple bond at 3. Right-to-left: triple bond at 2. Choose 2.}$

step_by_step.explanation: Identify substituent position with chosen numbering.
Assemble name: substituent + triple bond position + parent.

worked_examples.final_answer: 4-methylpent-2-yne. Triple bond position (2) takes priority over substituent position (4).
worked_examples.concept_applied: Triple bond numbering priority over substituents; alphabetical ordering for multiple substituents.

image.caption: 4-Methylpent-2-yne: 5-carbon parent with triple bond at C2 and methyl at C4.

worked_examples.title: Naming an Enyne (Double and Triple Bonds)

step_by_step.explanation: Identify parent chain containing both multiple bonds.
step_by_step.calculation: $\text{Chain: } 5 \text{ carbons with 1 double and 1 triple bond} \rightarrow \text{pentenyne}$

step_by_step.explanation: Number from end giving lowest position to nearest multiple bond.
step_by_step.calculation: $\text{Left-to-right: triple at 1, double at 4. Right-to-left: double at 1, triple at 4. Choose 1,4.}$

step_by_step.explanation: Apply enyne naming: 'e' from '-ene' dropped before '-yne'. List double bond position first in name.
Assemble complete name.

worked_examples.final_answer: pent-4-en-1-yne. Both multiple bond positions must be specified; '-en-yne' indicates one double and one triple bond.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Enyne nomenclature: double bond position listed before triple bond in name; number for lowest multiple bond position.

sections.introduction: Alkynes can be represented using various structural formats, each serving different purposes. Understanding these representations is essential for visualizing the linear geometry around triple bonds and communicating alkyne structures accurately. The triple bond is typically shown as three parallel lines in structural drawings.

categories.description: Shows only atom counts (e.g., $\text{C}_4\text{H}_6$ for butyne).
categories.detailed_mechanism: Molecular formulas indicate unsaturation but cannot distinguish between structural isomers. $\text{C}_4\text{H}_6$ could represent but-1-yne, but-2-yne, buta-1,3-diene, or cyclobutene. Additional structural information is needed to distinguish them.

examples.explanation: $\text{C}_4\text{H}_6$ represents multiple structures: but-1-yne, but-2-yne, buta-1,2-diene, buta-1,3-diene, cyclobutene. The degree of unsaturation is 2, but this doesn't specify whether from triple bond, two double bonds, or ring + double bond.

image.caption: $\text{C}_4\text{H}_6$ has multiple isomers: alkynes, dienes, and cyclic structures.

categories.description: Shows atom connectivity with triple bond indicated by '$\equiv$' (e.g., $\text{CH}_3\text{C}\equiv\text{CCH}_3$).
categories.detailed_mechanism: Condensed formulas show the sequence of atoms and triple bond position but don't clearly show the linear geometry. The '$\equiv$' symbol indicates the $\text{C}\equiv\text{C}$ triple bond location. Terminal alkynes show the acidic hydrogen explicitly.

examples.explanation: $\text{CH}_3\text{C}\equiv\text{CCH}_3$ shows 4-carbon chain with triple bond between carbons 2 and 3. Linear geometry is implied but not explicitly shown.

image.caption: Condensed formula shows connectivity with triple bond symbol.

examples.explanation: But-1-yne: $\text{HC}\equiv\text{CCH}_2\text{CH}_3$. The terminal hydrogen is shown explicitly to indicate the acidic $\text{C}-\text{H}$ bond characteristic of terminal alkynes.

categories.description: Shows all atoms and bonds explicitly, including the triple bond as three lines.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Lewis structures display every bond and atom, clearly showing the triple bond as three parallel lines. Useful for understanding bonding but becomes complex for larger molecules. All atoms attached to triple-bonded carbons should be drawn in a straight line.

examples.explanation: All 4 atoms shown with bonds: $\text{C}\equiv\text{C}$ triple bond (three lines) and two $\text{C}-\text{H}$ single bonds. All atoms lie on a straight line with $180^\circ$ bond angles.

image.caption: Ethyne Lewis structure showing all bonds and linear geometry.

categories.description: Simplified representation where the triple bond is shown as three parallel lines.
categories.detailed_mechanism: In skeletal structures: (1) vertices and endpoints = carbon atoms, (2) hydrogens on carbons are implied, (3) triple bonds shown as three parallel lines, (4) geometry around triple bond should be drawn as a straight line to reflect linear ($180^\circ$) structure. Terminal alkynes show the $\text{C}-\text{H}$ bond explicitly.

examples.explanation: Drawn as a line with triple line segment between carbons 3 and 4. The bonds leading into and out of the triple bond are drawn collinear (straight) to show linear geometry.

image.caption: Hex-3-yne skeletal: triple bond shown as three parallel lines with linear geometry.

examples.explanation: Pent-1-yne: triple bond at the end with explicit $\text{H}-\text{C}\equiv$ shown. The hydrogen on the terminal alkyne carbon is typically drawn to emphasize the acidic proton.

image.caption: Terminal alkyne skeletal: explicit H shown on triple-bonded carbon.

sections.introduction: Alkyne physical properties are similar to alkanes and alkenes of comparable molecular weight but show some distinctive trends due to the linear geometry and high electron density of the triple bond. Understanding these properties helps predict behavior in separation and purification processes.

categories.description: Alkyne boiling points increase with molecular weight, similar to alkanes and alkenes.
categories.detailed_mechanism: London dispersion forces dominate intermolecular attractions. Each $\text{CH}_2$ addition raises boiling point by $\approx 20-30^\circ\text{C}$. Alkynes have slightly higher boiling points than corresponding alkanes and alkenes due to increased polarizability from the two $\pi$-bond electron clouds and more linear shape allowing better packing.

examples.explanation: Ethyne ($-84^\circ\text{C}$, sublimes) < Propyne ($-23.2^\circ\text{C}$) < But-1-yne ($8.1^\circ\text{C}$) < Pent-1-yne ($40^\circ\text{C}$) < Hex-1-yne ($71^\circ\text{C}$). C2-C3 are gases; C4+ are liquids at room temperature.

image.alt: Graph of alkyne boiling points vs carbon number
image.caption: Alkyne boiling points increase with chain length, slightly higher than alkanes/alkenes.

categories.description: Terminal alkynes ($\text{R}-\text{C}\equiv\text{C}-\text{H}$) have different properties than internal alkynes ($\text{R}-\text{C}\equiv\text{C}-\text{R}'$).
categories.detailed_mechanism: Terminal alkynes have acidic hydrogens ($\text{p}K_a \approx 25$) that can be deprotonated by strong bases. This creates acetylide anions useful in synthesis. Terminal alkynes also have slightly lower boiling points than internal isomers due to reduced symmetry and weaker intermolecular forces.

examples.explanation: But-1-yne (terminal): $\text{BP} = 8.1^\circ\text{C}$, acidic H ($\text{p}K_a \approx 25$). But-2-yne (internal): $\text{BP} = 27^\circ\text{C}$, no acidic H. Internal alkyne has higher BP due to better symmetry and packing.

image.alt: Comparison of terminal and internal alkyne structures
image.caption: Terminal vs internal alkynes: different boiling points and reactivity.

categories.description: Alkynes are nonpolar and immiscible with water, similar to alkanes and alkenes.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Alkynes cannot form hydrogen bonds with water (except terminal alkynes which have weak H-bond donor capability). Densities range from $0.6-0.8 \text{ g/mL}$, less than water ($1.0 \text{ g/mL}$), so alkynes float on water. Soluble in nonpolar organic solvents (hexane, benzene, ether).

examples.explanation: Hex-1-yne ($\text{C}_6\text{H}_{10}$) forms separate layer on water. Density = $0.72 \text{ g/mL}$. Terminal alkynes show slight solubility in water due to weak H-bonding capability of acidic hydrogen.
examples.equation: $\text{Solubility in water: } < 0.1 \text{ g/100 mL (terminal slightly higher)}$

sections.introduction: One of the most distinctive chemical properties of alkynes is the acidity of terminal alkyne hydrogens. The $sp$-hybridized carbon in terminal alkynes holds electrons closer to the nucleus, making the $\text{C}-\text{H}$ bond more acidic than alkane or alkene $\text{C}-\text{H}$ bonds. This property enables unique synthetic transformations.

principles.statement: The acidity of $\text{C}-\text{H}$ bonds increases with increasing $s$-character of the carbon hybrid orbital: $sp$ (50% $s$) > $sp^2$ (33% $s$) > $sp^3$ (25% $s$).
principles.application: Terminal alkynes ($sp$ C-H) have $\text{p}K_a \approx 25$, making them much more acidic than alkenes ($sp^2$ C-H, $\text{p}K_a \approx 44$) or alkanes ($sp^3$ C-H, $\text{p}K_a \approx 50$). Strong bases like $\text{NaNH}_2$ can deprotonate terminal alkynes to form acetylide anions.
principles.analogy: Think of $s$-orbitals as being closer to the nucleus (like inner lanes on a track). More $s$-character means the bonding electrons are held tighter, making the hydrogen easier to remove as $\text{H}^+$. The $sp$ carbon is like a tighter grip on electrons.
principles.example: Ethyne ($\text{HC}\equiv\text{CH}$) reacts with $\text{NaNH}_2$ to form sodium acetylide ($\text{HC}\equiv\text{C}^-\text{Na}^+$) and $\text{NH}_3$. This reaction doesn't occur with ethene or ethane.

principles.statement: Terminal alkynes react with strong bases to form acetylide anions ($\text{R}-\text{C}\equiv\text{C}^-$), which are powerful nucleophiles for carbon-carbon bond formation.
principles.application: Acetylide anions are used in nucleophilic substitution reactions with alkyl halides to extend carbon chains. This is a key method for building larger alkyne molecules from smaller precursors.
principles.analogy: The acetylide anion is like a carbon-based 'arrow' that can shoot at electrophilic carbons (alkyl halides) to form new $\text{C}-\text{C}$ bonds. It's one of the few ways to directly connect carbon atoms in synthesis.

worked_examples.title: Deprotonation of a Terminal Alkyne
worked_examples.problem: Write the reaction equation for treating but-1-yne with sodium amide ($\text{NaNH}_2$) in liquid ammonia.

step_by_step.explanation: Identify the acidic hydrogen: terminal alkyne has acidic H on $sp$ carbon.
Identify the base: $\text{NaNH}_2$ provides $\text{NH}_2^-$ (strong base, conjugate base of $\text{NH}_3$, $\text{p}K_a \approx 38$).
Write acid-base reaction: $\text{NH}_2^-$ removes terminal H, forming acetylide anion and $\text{NH}_3$.
Include counterion ($\text{Na}^+$) for complete equation.

worked_examples.concept_applied: Terminal alkynes are acidic enough to be deprotonated by strong bases like $\text{NaNH}_2$, forming nucleophilic acetylide anions.

image.caption: Terminal alkyne deprotonation: $\text{NaNH}_2$ removes acidic H to form acetylide anion.

sections.key_concept: Terminal alkynes ($\text{R}-\text{C}\equiv\text{C}-\text{H}$) are acidic ($\text{p}K_a \approx 25$) due to $sp$ hybridization. Strong bases ($\text{NaNH}_2$, $\text{NaH}$, $\text{RLi}$) deprotonate to form acetylide anions ($\text{R}-\text{C}\equiv\text{C}^-$), powerful nucleophiles for $\text{C}-\text{C}$ bond formation.

sections.introduction: Several simple alkynes have major industrial importance as feedstocks for chemical synthesis, welding fuels, and polymer precursors. Understanding these common alkynes provides context for why alkyne chemistry is practically significant in industrial and laboratory settings.

components.function: Welding and cutting torches (oxyacetylene); chemical synthesis feedstock; vinyl chloride production
components.key_feature: Simplest alkyne; burns at $3300^\circ\text{C}$ in oxygen; global production ~15 million tons/year; stored dissolved in acetone for safety

components.function: Welding fuel (MAPP gas); chemical intermediate; semiconductor manufacturing
components.key_feature: Higher boiling point than ethyne ($-23.2^\circ\text{C}$); safer handling than acetylene; used in specialty welding applications

components.function: Chemical synthesis intermediates; polymer precursors; research applications
components.key_feature: Two isomers exist (terminal and internal); but-1-yne is acidic, but-2-yne is not; $\text{BP} = 8.1^\circ\text{C}$ and $27^\circ\text{C}$ respectively

components.function: Organic synthesis building block; click chemistry applications; pharmaceutical intermediates
components.key_feature: Aromatic alkyne; terminal H is acidic; used in Sonogashira coupling reactions; $\text{BP} = 142^\circ\text{C}$

components.function: Polymer synthesis; molecular electronics; porous materials
components.key_feature: Two terminal alkyne groups on benzene ring; used to create conjugated polymers and molecular wires

components.function: Biological defense compounds; pheromones; natural product synthesis
components.key_feature: Found in some plants and marine organisms; often toxic or biologically active; example: ichthyothereol from Asteraceae plants

sections.introduction: Advanced alkyne nomenclature involves handling multiple triple bonds, combined double and triple bonds, and substituents. This section provides systematic practice with increasingly complex structures to build naming proficiency for all alkyne types.

worked_examples.title: Naming with Multiple Substituents

step_by_step.explanation: Find longest chain containing the triple bond.
Number for lowest triple bond position.
step_by_step.calculation: $\text{Left-to-right: triple bond at 4. Right-to-left: triple bond at 2. Choose 2.}$

step_by_step.explanation: Identify substituent positions with chosen numbering.
step_by_step.calculation: $\text{Two methyl groups at position 4 (with right-to-left numbering)}$

step_by_step.explanation: Assemble name with substituent and triple bond position.

worked_examples.final_answer: 4,4-dimethylhex-2-yne. Triple bond at position 2; two methyl groups at position 4.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Triple bond position priority over substituents; use 'di-' prefix for identical substituents; list all position numbers.

image.caption: 4,4-Dimethylhex-2-yne: 6-carbon parent with triple bond at C2 and two methyls at C4.

worked_examples.title: Naming a Diyne (Two Triple Bonds)

step_by_step.explanation: Identify parent chain containing both triple bonds.
Number from end giving lowest set of triple bond positions.
Use '-diyne' suffix for two triple bonds; specify both positions.
Assemble complete name.

worked_examples.final_answer: hexa-1,5-diyne. Two terminal triple bonds at positions 1 and 5; symmetric structure.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Multiple triple bonds: '-diyne' suffix; all positions specified; both terminal hydrogens are acidic.

image.caption: Hexa-1,5-diyne: symmetric diyne with terminal triple bonds at both ends.

worked_examples.title: From Name to Structure: 5-Ethyl-3-Methylhept-3-yne

step_by_step.explanation: Identify parent chain from base name: 'hept-3-yne' = 7 carbons with triple bond at position 3.
Number the chain from left to right for drawing.
Add substituents at specified positions: methyl at C3, ethyl at C5.
Add hydrogens to satisfy tetravalency (each carbon needs 4 bonds).
Verify: count total carbons = 7 (parent) + 1 (methyl) + 2 (ethyl) = 10 carbons.

worked_examples.final_answer: Condensed structure: $\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{C}(\text{CH}_3)\equiv\text{CCH}(\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_3)\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_3$. Molecular formula: $\text{C}_{10}\text{H}_{18}$ (follows $\text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n-2}$ for one triple bond).
worked_examples.concept_applied: Name-to-structure conversion: parent chain with triple bond first, then add substituents at specified positions, then saturate with hydrogens.

image.caption: 5-Ethyl-3-methylhept-3-yne: heptyne parent with methyl at C3 and ethyl at C5.

worked_examples.title: Naming an Enyne with Substituents

step_by_step.explanation: Identify parent chain containing both multiple bonds.
step_by_step.calculation: $\text{Chain: } 6 \text{ carbons with 1 double and 1 triple bond} \rightarrow \text{hexenyne}$

step_by_step.explanation: Number from end giving lowest position to nearest multiple bond.
step_by_step.calculation: $\text{Left-to-right: double at 2, triple at 4. Right-to-left: triple at 2, double at 4. Both give 2,4.}$

step_by_step.explanation: Tiebreaker: double bond gets lower number when equidistant.
Assemble name: double bond position first, then triple bond.

worked_examples.final_answer: hex-2-en-4-yne. Double bond takes priority in tiebreaker; positions 2 and 4 for double and triple bonds respectively.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Enyne nomenclature tiebreaker: when multiple bonds are equidistant from ends, double bond receives lower numbering.

image.caption: Hex-2-en-4-yne: enyne with double bond at C2 and triple bond at C4.

sections.section_title: Alkyne Nomenclature Summary and Common Mistakes
sections.introduction: A consolidated summary of alkyne naming rules helps prevent common errors. This section highlights critical rules, frequent mistakes, and provides a quick-reference checklist for naming any alkyne structure, including comparison with alkane and alkene nomenclature.

principles.principle: Alkyne vs Alkene vs Alkane Naming Comparison
principles.statement: Alkyne nomenclature differs from alkane nomenclature in three key ways: suffix (-yne vs -ane), parent chain selection (must contain triple bond), and numbering priority (triple bond over substituents). Rules are identical to alkene nomenclature, substituting -yne for -ene.
principles.application: When naming any hydrocarbon, first identify the functional group. If a triple bond exists, apply alkyne rules even if a longer alkane chain is present. For molecules with both double and triple bonds, use enyne rules.
principles.analogy: Alkane naming is like naming a street by its length. Alkene/alkyne naming is like naming a street by its landmark (the multiple bond)—the landmark takes priority over length.
principles.example: Alkane: 3-methylhexane (longest chain = 6). Alkyne: 4-methylhex-2-yne (chain must contain triple bond, numbered for triple bond position).

principles.statement: Current IUPAC recommendations place the triple bond position immediately before the '-yne' suffix (e.g., pent-2-yne), though the older format (2-pentyne) remains acceptable.
principles.application: Use pent-2-yne format for consistency with modern IUPAC standards. For multiple triple bonds: penta-1,3-diyne (all positions before suffix). For enynes: pent-3-en-1-yne (double bond position first).
principles.analogy: Think of the position number as a label attached directly to the '-yne' flag: 'pent-2-yne' means 'pentane with -yne flag at position 2'.

categories.description: Choosing the longest chain instead of the chain containing the triple bond.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Students often default to alkane rules and select the absolute longest carbon chain. For alkynes, the chain MUST contain the triple bond even if shorter.

examples.explanation: INCORRECT: 2-propylpent-1-yne (7-carbon chain selected). CORRECT: 3-methylenehex-1-yne or proper naming with 6-carbon chain containing triple bond. The triple bond must be in the parent.

categories.description: Numbering for lowest substituent position instead of lowest triple bond position.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Alkane habit of minimizing substituent numbers conflicts with alkyne rule of minimizing triple bond numbers. Triple bond position always takes priority.

examples.explanation: INCORRECT: 4-methylpent-3-yne (numbered for methyl at 2). CORRECT: 2-methylpent-2-yne (numbered for triple bond at 2). Triple bond position 2 < 3, so this wins.

categories.type: Common Mistake: Missing Triple Bond Position
categories.description: Omitting the triple bond position number for alkynes with 4+ carbons.
categories.detailed_mechanism: For ethyne and propyne, position numbers are unnecessary (only one possible position). For butyne and larger, the position MUST be specified to distinguish isomers.

examples.explanation: INCORRECT: pentyne (ambiguous—which position?). CORRECT: pent-1-yne or pent-2-yne (specific position required). Butyne has two isomers; pentyne has multiple isomers.

categories.type: Common Mistake: Incorrect Suffix for Multiple Triple Bonds
categories.description: Using '-yne' instead of '-diyne' or '-triyne' for multiple triple bonds.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Each triple bond must be accounted for in the suffix. Two triple bonds = '-diyne'; three = '-triyne'. All positions must be specified.

examples.explanation: INCORRECT: pent-1,3-yne (wrong suffix). CORRECT: penta-1,3-diyne (correct suffix for two triple bonds). The 'di-' indicates two triple bonds.

categories.description: Incorrect numbering when both double and triple bonds are present.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Number for the lowest multiple bond position first. If equidistant, double bond gets lower number. In the name, list double bond position before triple bond position.

examples.explanation: INCORRECT: hex-4-en-2-yne (triple bond numbered lower in tie). CORRECT: hex-2-en-4-yne (double bond wins tiebreaker, listed first in name).

components.composition: Longest continuous chain containing the triple bond
components.key_feature: Must contain triple bond even if shorter chain exists without it

components.composition: Assign 1, 2, 3... from end nearest triple bond
components.function: Gives triple bond the lowest possible position number
components.key_feature: Triple bond position priority over substituent positions

components.composition: Alkyl groups: methyl, ethyl, propyl, etc.
components.function: Identifies all branches off parent chain
components.key_feature: Use di-, tri-, tetra- for multiples; alphabetize substituents
Commas between numbers; hyphens between numbers and letters; no spaces

components.composition: Both double and triple bonds present
components.function: Uses '-en-yne' suffix with specific numbering rules
components.key_feature: Double bond position listed first; double bond wins tiebreaker

summary: Alkynes are unsaturated hydrocarbons containing at least one $\text{C}\equiv\text{C}$ triple bond, with general formula $\text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n-2}$ for acyclic mono-alkynes. Each triple-bonded carbon is $sp$ hybridized with linear geometry ($180^\circ$ bond angles). The triple bond consists of one $\sigma$-bond (strong, head-on overlap) and two $\pi$-bonds (weaker, perpendicular $p$-orbital overlaps). The two $\pi$-bonds create cylindrical electron density around the bond axis, making alkynes more reactive than alkanes and alkenes. IUPAC nomenclature requires: (1) parent chain must contain the triple bond, (2) numbering prioritizes lowest triple bond position over substituent positions, (3) suffix '-yne' replaces '-ane', (4) triple bond position specified for $\text{C}_4+$ alkynes (e.g., pent-2-yne). Multiple triple bonds use '-diyne', '-triyne' suffixes with all positions numbered. When both double and triple bonds exist, use '-enyne' suffix with double bond position listed first; double bond wins numbering tiebreaker. Terminal alkynes ($\text{R}-\text{C}\equiv\text{C}-\text{H}$) have acidic hydrogens ($\text{p}K_a \approx 25$) due to $sp$ hybridization, forming acetylide anions with strong bases—key intermediates for $\text{C}-\text{C}$ bond formation. Common alkynes include ethyne (welding, chemical feedstock), propyne (MAPP gas), and phenylacetylene (synthesis). Physical properties resemble alkanes but with slightly higher boiling points. Critical naming mistakes to avoid: selecting wrong parent chain (must contain triple bond), wrong numbering direction (triple bond priority), missing position numbers, incorrect suffixes for multiple triple bonds, and enyne numbering errors.
